topics.title: Approach to the Patient with Renal Disease
topics.content:
Overview of Renal Syndromes

The kidney performs critical functions: filtration, reabsorption, secretion, endocrine activity (EPO, renin, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D), and acid-base/electrolyte homeostasis. Renal diseases are classified by the predominant clinical syndrome they produce.

Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) — Classification by Etiology
CategoryKey FeaturesFENaUrine OsmolalitySediment
PrerenalHypoperfusion (hypovolemia, heart failure, sepsis)<1%>500 mOsm/kgBland, hyaline casts
Intrinsic RenalParenchymal damage (ATN, GN, TIN)>2% (ATN)~300 mOsm/kgMuddy brown casts (ATN), RBC casts (GN), WBC casts (TIN)
PostrenalObstruction (BPH, stones, tumor)VariableVariableMay be bland
Nephritic Syndromes

Characterized by hematuria (often with RBC casts), hypertension, oliguria, and mild-to-moderate proteinuria (<3.5 g/day).

Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN) — Classification:

TypeMechanismSerologyExamples
Type IAnti-GBM antibodiesAnti-GBM (+)Goodpasture's syndrome
Type IIImmune complexLow C3/C4, ANA, anti-dsDNA, ASOLupus nephritis, PSGN, IgA nephropathy
Type IIIPauci-immune (ANCA-associated)ANCA (+): c-ANCA (PR3) or p-ANCA (MPO)GPA, MPA, EGPA
Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
  • Follows group A strep pharyngitis (1-3 weeks) or skin infection (3-6 weeks)
  • Low C3, elevated ASO and anti-DNase B titers
  • Light microscopy: diffuse proliferative GN with "starry sky" pattern (subepithelial humps on EM)
  • Usually self-limited; supportive treatment
Nephrotic Syndrome

Defined by proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia, and lipiduria (oval fat bodies, Maltese cross under polarized light).

CauseKey FeaturesTreatment
Minimal Change Disease (MCD)Most common in children; nil lesion on LM; foot process effacement on EMCorticosteroids (highly responsive)
Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)Most common primary nephrotic syndrome in adults (especially African Americans — APOL1 risk alleles); segmental sclerosis on LMCorticosteroids, cyclophosphamide, calcineurin inhibitors
Membranous Nephropathy (MGN)Most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in white adults; anti-PLA2R antibodies (~70%); subepithelial deposits ("spike and dome")Rituximab, cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids
Diabetic NephropathyKimmelstiel-Wilson nodular glomerulosclerosis; progressive microalbuminuria → overt proteinuriaACEi/ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors, glycemic control
Tubulointerstitial Nephritis (TIN)
  • Acute allergic TIN: Drug-induced (NSAIDs, antibiotics — penicillins, cephalosporins, rifampin, ciprofloxacin, PPIs); classic triad of fever, rash, eosinophilia (often incomplete)
  • Immune-mediated TIN: Sarcoidosis, Sjögren's, IgG4-related disease, TINU syndrome
  • Infection-associated TIN: Pyelonephritis, viral (CMV, BK, HIV)
Hereditary and Congenital Renal Diseases
DiseaseGene/ProteinKey Features
ADPKDPKD1/PKD2 (polycystin-1/2)Bilateral cysts, HTN, berry aneurysms, hepatic cysts
Alport SyndromeCOL4A3/4/5 (type IV collagen)Hematuria, SNHL, lenticonus; X-linked most common (COL4A5)
Fabry DiseaseGLA (α-galactosidase A)X-linked; angiokeratomas, neuropathy, corneal verticillata
Thin Basement Membrane DiseaseCOL4A3/4 heterozygousBenign familial hematuria; excellent prognosis
CKD Staging (KDIGO)
StageGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)Description
G1≥90Normal or high (with evidence of kidney damage)
G260-89Mildly decreased
G3a45-59Mildly to moderately decreased
G3b30-44Moderately to severely decreased
G415-29Severely decreased
G5<15Kidney failure (ESKD)

Albuminuria categories: A1 (<30 mg/g), A2 (30-300 mg/g — moderately increased), A3 (>300 mg/g — severely increased)

Systemic Syndromes Involving the Kidney
  • Hepatorenal syndrome: Functional renal failure in advanced liver disease; type 1 (rapidly progressive) vs type 2 (slowly progressive with refractory ascites)
  • Cardiorenal syndrome: Bidirectional heart-kidney dysfunction; 5 types based on acute/chronic and primary organ
  • Pulmonary-renal syndromes: Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture's), ANCA vasculitis (GPA, MPA)
  • Tumor lysis syndrome: Hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia → AKI from crystal deposition; prevention with rasburicase, aggressive hydration
  • Atheroembolic renal disease: Cholesterol crystal embolization after vascular procedures; livedo reticularis, blue toe syndrome, eosinophilia, hypocomplementemia
  • Thrombotic microangiopathies (TMA): HUS, TTP, aHUS, malignant HTN

topics.title: Renal Physiology
topics.content:
Embryology and Nephron Structure

The kidney develops through three successive structures: pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros (definitive kidney, from week 5). The ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct) induces the metanephric mesenchyme to form nephrons.

Each kidney contains ~1 million nephrons:

  • Cortical nephrons (~85%): Short loops of Henle; primarily regulate GFR and filtration
  • Juxtamedullary nephrons (~15%): Long loops of Henle extending deep into medulla; critical for urine concentration
Glomerular Filtration

Normal GFR: ~120 mL/min (180 L/day); only ~1-2 L excreted as urine

GFR Regulation:

  • Myogenic reflex: Afferent arteriolar constriction in response to increased perfusion pressure (autoregulation)
  • Tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF): Macula densa senses NaCl delivery → signals afferent arteriole; increased NaCl → afferent constriction → decreased GFR
  • RAAS: Angiotensin II preferentially constricts efferent arteriole → maintains GFR in low-perfusion states
  • Autoregulation maintains GFR across MAP 80-180 mmHg
Segmental Nephron Transport
Segment% NaCl ReabsorbedKey TransportersDiuretic Target
Proximal Tubule~60%Na⁺/H⁺ exchanger (NHE3), SGLT2, Na⁺-K⁺-ATPaseAcetazolamide (CA inhibitor), SGLT2 inhibitors
Thick Ascending Limb (TAL)15-25%Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ cotransporter (NKCC2)Furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide (loop diuretics)
Distal Convoluted Tubule (DCT)~5%Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter (NCC)Hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone (thiazides)
Collecting Duct4-5%ENaC (principal cells), H⁺-ATPase (intercalated cells)Amiloride, triamterene, spironolactone, eplerenone
Countercurrent Multiplication and Urine Concentration
  • Descending limb of Henle: Permeable to water, relatively impermeable to solutes → water exits → tubular fluid becomes concentrated
  • Ascending limb (thin + thick): Impermeable to water; active NaCl reabsorption in TAL → tubular fluid becomes dilute; creates medullary hypertonicity
  • Countercurrent exchange in vasa recta preserves medullary osmotic gradient
  • ADH (vasopressin): Binds V2 receptors → inserts aquaporin-2 channels in collecting duct → water reabsorption → concentrated urine (up to ~1200 mOsm/kg)
  • Without ADH: dilute urine (~50-100 mOsm/kg)
Inherited Tubular Transport Disorders
DisorderSegmentDefectClinical Features
Bartter SyndromeTALNKCC2, ROMK, or ClC-KbHypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, hypercalciuria; mimics loop diuretic use
Gitelman SyndromeDCTNCC (thiazide-sensitive cotransporter)Hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, hypocalciuria, hypomagnesemia; mimics thiazide use
Liddle SyndromeCollecting ductGain-of-function ENaCHTN, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis; mimics hyperaldosteronism but low aldosterone
Pseudohypoaldosteronism Type ICollecting ductLoss-of-function ENaC or MRSalt wasting, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis
Nephrogenic DICollecting ductV2 receptor or AQP2 mutationsPolyuria, polydipsia; ADH-resistant
RTA Type I (Distal)Collecting duct (α-intercalated)H⁺-ATPase or AE1 defectNon-AG metabolic acidosis, urine pH >5.5, nephrocalcinosis, hypokalemia
RTA Type II (Proximal)Proximal tubuleNaHCO₃ reabsorption defectNon-AG metabolic acidosis, urine pH <5.5 when plasma HCO₃ below threshold; Fanconi syndrome
Fanconi SyndromeProximal tubuleGlobal proximal reabsorptive defectGlycosuria, aminoaciduria, phosphaturia, uricosuria, bicarbonaturia
Water and Sodium Balance
  • ADH release stimulated by: increased plasma osmolality (>285 mOsm/kg — osmoreceptors in hypothalamus), decreased effective arterial volume, nausea, pain
  • Aldosterone: Synthesized in zona glomerulosa; stimulated by angiotensin II and hyperkalemia; acts on principal cells → ENaC insertion → Na⁺ reabsorption, K⁺ and H⁺ secretion
  • ANP/BNP: Released by atrial/ventricular stretch → natriuresis, vasodilation, suppression of RAAS and ADH

topics.title: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)
topics.content:
Definition and Staging (KDIGO)
StageSerum Creatinine CriteriaUrine Output Criteria
1↑ ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h OR ↑ 1.5-1.9× baseline within 7 days<0.5 mL/kg/h for 6-12h
2↑ 2.0-2.9× baseline<0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥12h
3↑ ≥3.0× baseline OR SCr ≥4.0 mg/dL OR initiation of RRT<0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24h OR anuria ≥12h
Epidemiology
  • 5-7% of all hospital admissions; up to 30% of ICU admissions
  • Hospital-acquired AKI: most commonly from ATN (ischemic or nephrotoxic)
  • Community-acquired AKI: most commonly prerenal
  • Mortality in severe AKI requiring dialysis: 50-80% in ICU settings
Prerenal AKI

Caused by reduced renal perfusion without structural damage:

  • Hypovolemia: Hemorrhage, GI losses, burns, third-spacing
  • Decreased cardiac output: Heart failure, cardiogenic shock, massive PE
  • Systemic vasodilation: Sepsis, anaphylaxis, hepatorenal syndrome
  • Renal vasoconstriction: NSAIDs (block prostaglandin-mediated afferent dilation), ACEi/ARBs (block AII-mediated efferent constriction), calcineurin inhibitors, hypercalcemia
Key Point: Prerenal AKI is reversible if perfusion is restored promptly; prolonged ischemia → ATN
Intrinsic Renal AKI

Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) — most common cause of intrinsic AKI:

  • Ischemic ATN: Prolonged prerenal state → tubular cell injury/death; patchy necrosis especially in S3 segment (medullary thick ascending limb and proximal straight tubule)
  • Nephrotoxic ATN:
  • Aminoglycosides: Dose-dependent; nonoliguric; typically after 5-7 days; proximal tubule toxicity
  • Amphotericin B: Dose-dependent renal vasoconstriction + direct tubular toxicity
  • Cisplatin: Direct tubular toxicity + renal vasoconstriction
  • Vancomycin: Especially with concurrent aminoglycosides or piperacillin-tazobactam
  • Contrast-induced AKI: SCr rise within 24-48h, peaks 3-5 days; risk factors: CKD, diabetes, volume depletion, large contrast volume; prevention with IV normal saline hydration
  • Rhabdomyolysis: Myoglobin-induced tubular obstruction/toxicity; CK >5000; pigmented granular casts; treat with aggressive IV saline ± mannitol, alkalinization

Other intrinsic causes: Acute GN, acute TIN, vascular (TMA, renal artery thrombosis/embolism, renal vein thrombosis)

Postrenal AKI (Obstructive)
  • Must involve bilateral ureteral obstruction or bladder outlet obstruction (or unilateral in single functioning kidney)
  • Common causes: BPH (most common in older men), urolithiasis, malignancy (cervical, prostate, bladder), retroperitoneal fibrosis
  • Diagnosis: bladder catheterization → renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis)
Diagnostic Evaluation

Urinary Indices in AKI:

ParameterPrerenalATN
FENa<1%>2%
FEUrea<35%>35%
BUN/Cr ratio>20:1<15:1
Urine sodium<20 mEq/L>40 mEq/L
Urine osmolality>500 mOsm/kg~300 mOsm/kg
Urine specific gravity>1.020~1.010
Caveat: FENa unreliable with diuretic use → use FEUrea instead; FENa may be <1% in contrast nephropathy, myoglobinuria, early obstruction, acute GN

Urinary Sediment Interpretation:

  • Bland: Prerenal, postrenal
  • Muddy brown granular casts: ATN
  • RBC casts: Glomerulonephritis
  • WBC casts: Pyelonephritis, TIN
  • Eosinophiluria: AIN (low sensitivity), atheroembolic disease
  • Oxalate crystals: Ethylene glycol poisoning
  • Uric acid crystals: Tumor lysis syndrome

Novel Biomarkers: KIM-1 (kidney injury molecule-1), NGAL (neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin), TIMP-2 × IGFBP7 — detect tubular injury before SCr rises

Management
  1. Treat underlying cause (restore perfusion, remove nephrotoxins, relieve obstruction)
  2. Volume management: IV fluids for prerenal; diuretics for volume overload (no evidence furosemide prevents or treats ATN, but manages fluid balance)
  3. Electrolyte management:
  • Hyperkalemia: Calcium gluconate (membrane stabilization), insulin + glucose, sodium bicarbonate, kayexalate/patiromer/SZC, emergent dialysis if refractory
  • Metabolic acidosis: Sodium bicarbonate if pH <7.1-7.2; dialysis if severe
  • Hyperphosphatemia: Phosphate binders, dietary restriction
  1. Avoid further nephrotoxic insult: Hold NSAIDs, aminoglycosides; adjust drug doses for GFR
  2. Nutrition: Adequate caloric intake (20-30 kcal/kg/day); protein 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day (higher if on CRRT)
Dialysis Indications in AKI (mnemonic: AEIOU)
  • Acidosis: Severe metabolic acidosis refractory to bicarbonate
  • Electrolytes: Refractory hyperkalemia
  • Ingestion/Intoxication: Dialyzable toxins (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates)
  • Overload: Volume overload refractory to diuretics (pulmonary edema)
  • Uremia: Symptomatic uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)

CRRT vs Intermittent HD: CRRT preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients (slower fluid/solute removal → better tolerated); no mortality difference in RCTs

topics.title: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
topics.content:
Definition and Classification

CKD is defined as kidney damage or GFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² for ≥3 months. KDIGO classifies by GFR (G1-G5) and albuminuria (A1-A3) — see staging table in Chapter 308.

Epidemiology and Risk Factors
  • Prevalence: ~10-13% of adult population worldwide
  • Leading causes vary by region: diabetic nephropathy (most common in developed countries), hypertensive nephrosclerosis, glomerulonephritis (most common worldwide), ADPKD, TIN

Risk Factors:

  • Non-modifiable: Age, family history, race (African American — APOL1 variants), low birth weight, male sex
  • Modifiable: Diabetes, hypertension, obesity, smoking, nephrotoxin exposure, high dietary protein/sodium
  • Genetic: >60 monogenic risk loci identified; key genes include APOL1 (FSGS, HIVAN), PKD1/PKD2 (ADPKD), COL4A3/4/5 (Alport), UMOD (ADTKD)
Pathophysiology

Hyperfiltration hypothesis (Brenner): Loss of nephrons → compensatory hyperfiltration in remaining nephrons → glomerular hypertension → progressive glomerulosclerosis → further nephron loss (vicious cycle)

Key mediators of progression:

  • Angiotensin II: Efferent arteriolar constriction → glomerular HTN; profibrotic, proinflammatory
  • Proteinuria: Direct tubular toxicity; marker AND mediator of progression
  • TGF-β: Master fibrogenic cytokine → tubulointerstitial fibrosis (final common pathway)
GFR Estimation
EquationNotes
CKD-EPI (2021)Preferred; uses creatinine ± cystatin C; race-free equation
MDRDOlder; underestimates GFR at higher levels
Cockcroft-GaultEstimates CrCl (not GFR); used for drug dosing; includes weight
Cystatin C-basedLess affected by muscle mass; useful when creatinine unreliable

24-hour urine CrCl: Gold standard but impractical; useful in extremes of body size, muscle wasting, vegetarian diet

Leading Etiologies of CKD
EtiologyKey FeaturesSpecific Markers
Diabetic NephropathyMicroalbuminuria → macroalbuminuria → declining GFR; retinopathy often concurrentHbA1c, urine albumin/creatinine ratio
Hypertensive NephrosclerosisSlowly progressive; African Americans disproportionately affected (APOL1)Benign arteriosclerosis on biopsy
GlomerulonephritisVariable presentation; may present as nephrotic or nephriticComplement levels, autoantibodies, biopsy
ADPKDLarge echogenic kidneys with multiple cystsFamily history, ultrasound, genetic testing
Chronic TINReflux nephropathy, analgesic nephropathy, lithium, leadSmall kidneys, bland sediment
Complications of CKD
Fluid, Electrolyte, and Acid-Base Disorders
  • Sodium and water: Impaired concentrating and diluting ability; volume overload in advanced CKD
  • Potassium: Hyperkalemia in advanced CKD (GFR <20); adaptive increase in colonic K⁺ secretion; worsened by ACEi/ARBs, spironolactone, trimethoprim, high K⁺ diet, metabolic acidosis
  • Metabolic acidosis: Non-anion gap initially (reduced NH₄⁺ excretion → Type IV RTA), then anion gap (retained organic acids — sulfate, phosphate, urate) in advanced CKD; contributes to muscle wasting, bone disease, progression
CKD-Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD)

Pathophysiology cascade:

  1. ↓ GFR → ↓ phosphate excretion → hyperphosphatemia
  2. ↓ 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production (reduced 1α-hydroxylase activity)
  3. ↓ Calcium absorption → hypocalcemia
  4. ↑ FGF-23 (earliest biomarker; phosphaturic hormone from osteocytes)
  5. Secondary hyperparathyroidism → high-turnover bone disease

Bone Disease Types in CKD:

TypePTHBone TurnoverFeatures
Osteitis fibrosa cystica↑↑↑HighSubperiosteal resorption, "salt and pepper" skull, brown tumors
Adynamic bone diseaseLow-normalLowOver-suppression of PTH (excess calcium/vitamin D); fracture risk
OsteomalaciaVariableLowAluminum toxicity (historical), vitamin D deficiency; Looser zones
Mixed uremic osteodystrophy↑VariableCombination features

Management: Phosphate binders (calcium carbonate, sevelamer, lanthanum), calcitriol or analogs (paricalcitol), calcimimetics (cinacalcet, etelcalcetide); target PTH 2-9× upper normal in CKD G5

Cardiovascular Disease
  • Leading cause of death in CKD — accounts for ~50% of deaths
  • Risk 10-200× higher than age-matched general population
  • Uremic cardiomyopathy: LVH (pressure + volume overload), diastolic dysfunction
  • Accelerated atherosclerosis: Traditional + non-traditional risk factors (hyperphosphatemia, secondary HPT, chronic inflammation, oxidative stress)
  • Vascular calcification: Metastatic calcification from Ca × P product elevation; coronary artery calcification, valvular calcification
  • Pericardial disease: Uremic pericarditis (indication for urgent dialysis); dialysis-associated pericarditis
  • HTN management: Target <130/80 mmHg; ACEi/ARBs first-line (reduce proteinuria, slow progression); monitor K⁺ and SCr after initiation (up to 30% rise in SCr acceptable)
Hematologic Abnormalities
  • Anemia: Normocytic, normochromic; primarily from ↓ EPO production; also iron deficiency (blood loss, impaired absorption), shortened RBC survival, uremic inhibitors of erythropoiesis
  • Target Hgb: 100-115 g/L (avoid >130 — increased cardiovascular events per TREAT, CHOIR, CREATE trials)
  • Treatment: Iron supplementation first (target ferritin >100, TSAT >20%); then ESAs (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin alfa)

Other causes of anemia in CKD: Iron deficiency, folate/B12 deficiency, hyperparathyroidism (marrow fibrosis), aluminum toxicity, chronic inflammation, ACEi-induced (rare)

Neuromuscular Complications
  • CNS: Uremic encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis, seizures — indication for dialysis), cognitive impairment, sleep disorders
  • Peripheral neuropathy: Distal symmetric sensorimotor; "burning feet syndrome"; improves with adequate dialysis
  • Restless legs syndrome: Common; responds to gabapentin, dopamine agonists
  • Myopathy: Proximal muscle weakness; related to vitamin D deficiency, electrolyte abnormalities
GI and Nutritional
  • Uremic fetor (ammonia breath), metallic taste, anorexia, nausea, vomiting
  • GI bleeding: Uremic platelet dysfunction (defective vWF interaction); treat with desmopressin (DDAVP), conjugated estrogens
  • Protein-energy wasting: Multifactorial (anorexia, acidosis, inflammation, dialysis losses); protein intake 0.6-0.8 g/kg/day in non-dialysis CKD, 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day on dialysis
Endocrine-Metabolic
  • Glucose intolerance: Peripheral insulin resistance (uremic toxins); hypoglycemia risk from reduced renal insulin clearance
  • Dyslipidemia: ↑ Triglycerides, ↓ HDL, ↑ Lp(a); statins reduce cardiovascular events (SHARP trial)
  • Sexual dysfunction: ↓ Testosterone, ↓ libido, erectile dysfunction, menstrual irregularities, infertility
  • Thyroid: Low T3 syndrome (euthyroid sick); typically does not require treatment
  • Growth retardation: In children with CKD; treated with recombinant GH
Dermatologic
  • Uremic pruritus: Very common; multifactorial (xerosis, secondary HPT, uremic toxins, neuropathic); treat with emollients, gabapentin, nalfurafine (κ-opioid agonist), difelikefalin, UV-B phototherapy
  • Uremic frost: Rare; white crystalline deposits on skin in severe untreated uremia
  • Nephrogenic systemic fibrosis: Associated with gadolinium-based contrast in GFR <30; thickened skin, joint contractures; avoid gadolinium in severe CKD
  • Calciphylaxis: Calcific uremic arteriolopathy; painful violaceous skin lesions → necrotic ulcers; associated with warfarin, obesity, high Ca × P product; treat with sodium thiosulfate
Management of CKD Progression

Slowing progression:

  • ACEi/ARBs: Reduce proteinuria and intraglomerular pressure; cornerstone of therapy
  • SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin): Reduce hyperfiltration (TGF mechanism); proven benefit in diabetic AND non-diabetic CKD (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY trials)
  • Finerenone (non-steroidal MRA): Reduces cardiovascular events and CKD progression in diabetic kidney disease (FIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD)
  • Blood pressure control: <130/80; reduce dietary sodium to <2g/day
  • Glycemic control: HbA1c <7% (but avoid <6.5% due to hypoglycemia risk with reduced renal clearance)
  • Avoid nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, IV contrast (if necessary: hydrate, use low-osmolar/iso-osmolar agents, minimize volume)
  • Treat acidosis: Oral sodium bicarbonate to maintain HCO₃ ≥22 mEq/L
  • Dietary protein restriction: 0.6-0.8 g/kg/day in CKD G3-5 (not on dialysis)

Preparation for RRT: Begins at CKD G4; options include hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, kidney transplantation (preferred), conservative/palliative management. AV fistula creation recommended 6 months before anticipated dialysis start.

topics.title: Dialysis in the Treatment of Renal Failure
topics.content:
Hemodialysis (HD)

Principles:

  • Diffusion: Solute movement across semipermeable membrane down concentration gradient (small molecules: urea, creatinine, K⁺)
  • Convection (ultrafiltration): Bulk water removal driven by transmembrane pressure gradient; removes middle molecules
  • Dialysate: Typically contains Na⁺ 135-145, K⁺ 0-4, Ca²⁺ 2.5-3.5, HCO₃⁻ 30-38 mEq/L, glucose 200 mg/dL

Vascular Access (in order of preference):

  1. AV fistula (preferred): Native vessel; lowest infection/thrombosis rate; needs 2-6 months to mature; "Rule of 6s" — flow >600 mL/min, diameter >6mm, depth <6mm, length >6cm
  2. AV graft: Synthetic (ePTFE); usable in 2-4 weeks; higher thrombosis/infection than fistula
  3. Central venous catheter: Temporary or tunneled; highest infection rate; associated with central venous stenosis; use only as bridge

Adequacy: Measured by Kt/V (target ≥1.2 per session for thrice-weekly) or urea reduction ratio (URR) (target ≥65-70%); K = dialyzer clearance, t = time, V = volume of distribution of urea

Standard regimen: 3-4 hours, 3 times/week; alternative: short daily HD, long nocturnal HD (may have better outcomes)

Complications:

  • Intradialytic hypotension (most common): Excessive ultrafiltration rate, autonomic neuropathy, antihypertensives; manage with reduced UF rate, sodium modeling, midodrine
  • Muscle cramps: Related to volume contraction, electrolyte shifts
  • Dialyzer reactions: Type A (anaphylactoid — ethylene oxide sensitivity, AN69 membranes + ACEi) and Type B (chest/back pain, complement activation)
  • Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome: Cerebral edema from rapid urea removal → headache, nausea, seizures; more common in first sessions; prevent with shorter, slower initial treatments
  • Amyloidosis (dialysis-related): β2-microglobulin deposition; carpal tunnel syndrome, bone cysts, spondyloarthropathy; seen after >7-10 years on HD
  • Air embolism: Rare but potentially fatal
  • Infections: Catheter-related bloodstream infections (S. aureus most common); AV graft/fistula infections
Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)

Principle: Peritoneal membrane serves as semipermeable dialysis membrane; dialysate instilled via Tenckhoff catheter into peritoneal cavity

Modalities:

  • CAPD (Continuous Ambulatory PD): 4 exchanges/day, each dwelling 4-6 hours (8-12 hours overnight); most common worldwide
  • CCPD/APD (Continuous Cycling/Automated PD): Cycler performs exchanges overnight; more convenient for employed patients

Peritoneal Equilibration Test (PET): Characterizes peritoneal membrane transport:

  • High transporters: Rapid solute equilibration but rapid glucose absorption → poor ultrafiltration; best suited for APD (short dwells)
  • Low transporters: Slow equilibration but good sustained UF; best suited for CAPD (long dwells)
  • High-average and Low-average: Most common; appropriate for either modality

Adequacy: Weekly Kt/V ≥1.7 (combined peritoneal + residual renal)

Complications:

  • Peritonitis (most serious): Cloudy effluent, abdominal pain, WBC >100/μL with >50% PMNs; most common organisms: gram-positive cocci (S. epidermidis > S. aureus); treat with intraperitoneal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime/gentamicin); fungal peritonitis → catheter removal
  • Exit site/tunnel infections: S. aureus most common; topical mupirocin prophylaxis
  • Hernias: Increased intra-abdominal pressure
  • Encapsulating peritoneal sclerosis: Rare but devastating; peritoneal fibrosis → bowel obstruction; associated with long-term PD (>5-8 years)
  • Metabolic: Hyperglycemia (glucose absorption from dialysate), hypertriglyceridemia, protein losses (5-15 g/day), weight gain
  • Hydrothorax: Dialysate leak through diaphragmatic defect (usually right-sided)
Long-Term Outcomes in ESKD
  • Cardiovascular disease: Leading cause of death (~40-50%); accelerated atherosclerosis, sudden cardiac death
  • 5-year survival: ~35-40% for dialysis patients (worse than many cancers)
  • Kidney transplantation: Best long-term outcomes; ~2× survival advantage over remaining on dialysis waitlist

topics.title: Transplantation in the Treatment of Renal Failure
topics.content:
Donor Types

Deceased donors:

  • Brain-dead donors (DBD): Traditional; standard criteria or expanded criteria donors (ECD)
  • Donation after circulatory death (DCD): Increasing use; higher delayed graft function rate
DCD CategoryDescription
IDead on arrival
IIUnsuccessful resuscitation
IIIAwaiting cardiac arrest (controlled — most common for transplant)
IVCardiac arrest after brain death
VCardiac arrest in hospitalized patient

Living donors: Better outcomes; preemptive transplantation (before dialysis) is ideal

  • KDPI (Kidney Donor Profile Index): Percentile ranking of deceased donor kidney quality (0-100%); lower = better
Outcomes
MetricDeceased DonorLiving Donor
1-year graft survival~93%~98%
1-year patient survival~96%~99%
10-year graft survival~48%~65%
Graft half-life~12-14 years~20 years
Tissue Typing and Immunogenetics
  • HLA system: Major histocompatibility complex on chromosome 6; HLA-A, -B, -C (Class I) and HLA-DR, -DQ, -DP (Class II)
  • Crossmatch: Recipient serum tested against donor lymphocytes; positive crossmatch = absolute contraindication (hyperacute rejection risk)
  • Panel Reactive Antibody (PRA/cPRA): Percentage of population to which recipient has preformed antibodies; higher PRA = longer wait time
  • Desensitization: For sensitized patients — plasmapheresis, IVIg, rituximab, bortezomib
Immunology of Rejection
TypeTimingMechanismPathologyTreatment
HyperacuteMinutes-hoursPreformed donor-specific antibodiesThrombosis, infarctionGraft nephrectomy (irreversible)
Acute T-cell mediatedDays-monthsT-cell infiltrationTubulitis, interstitial inflammationPulse methylprednisolone; ATG if steroid-resistant
Acute antibody-mediatedDays-monthsDonor-specific antibodies (DSA)Peritubular capillaritis, C4d depositionPlasmapheresis, IVIg, rituximab, eculizumab
Chronic (IFTA)Months-yearsMixed immune + non-immuneInterstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophyOptimize immunosuppression; treat contributing factors

Banff Classification: International standardized grading system for renal allograft pathology

Maintenance Immunosuppression
DrugMechanismKey Side Effects
GlucocorticoidsAnti-inflammatory, lymphocyte apoptosisDiabetes, osteoporosis, weight gain, cataracts, AVN, Cushingoid
CyclosporineCalcineurin inhibitor (IL-2 suppression)Nephrotoxicity, HTN, hyperlipidemia, hirsutism, gingival hyperplasia, tremor
TacrolimusCalcineurin inhibitor (more potent)Nephrotoxicity, diabetes (more than CsA), neurotoxicity, alopecia
Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF)IMPDH inhibitor (purine synthesis)GI (diarrhea, nausea), leukopenia; teratogenic
AzathioprinePurine analog (older; largely replaced by MMF)Leukopenia, hepatotoxicity; interacts with allopurinol (must reduce dose 75%)
Sirolimus/EverolimusmTOR inhibitorHyperlipidemia, thrombocytopenia, impaired wound healing, pneumonitis, oral ulcers; NOT nephrotoxic
BelataceptCostimulation blocker (CD80/86-CD28)Higher acute rejection rate; risk of PTLD (especially EBV-seronegative); IV monthly

Standard regimen: Tacrolimus + mycophenolate + prednisone (with anti-IL-2R or ATG induction)

Post-Transplant Management

High-risk (sensitized, repeat transplant, DCD, high KDPI): ATG induction → triple maintenance

Low-risk (living donor, well-matched): Basiliximab induction → triple maintenance; consider steroid withdrawal

Post-Transplant Infections — Timeline
PeriodCommon Infections
<1 monthSurgical site infections, UTI, pneumonia, C. difficile; donor-derived infections
1-6 monthsCMV (most common opportunistic — prophylaxis with valganciclovir), BK polyomavirus (nephropathy), Pneumocystis jirovecii (prophylaxis with TMP-SMX ×6 months), EBV, HSV/VZV
>6 monthsCommunity-acquired infections; late CMV; Pneumocystis if off prophylaxis; JC virus (PML)

BK Virus Nephropathy: "Decoy cells" in urine; plasma BK DNA >10,000 copies/mL; treatment = reduce immunosuppression; no specific antiviral

Post-Transplant Malignancy
  • Overall malignancy incidence: 5-6% (3-5× general population)
  • Skin cancer: Most common (SCC > BCC; reversed from general population)
  • PTLD (Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder): EBV-driven; B-cell proliferation; treatment = reduce immunosuppression, rituximab ± chemotherapy
  • Kaposi's sarcoma: HHV-8 associated; switch to sirolimus (anti-angiogenic)
Other Post-Transplant Complications
  • Cardiovascular disease: Leading cause of death with functioning graft; aggressive risk factor management
  • Diabetes (NODAT/PTDM): ~20-30%; risk factors: tacrolimus, steroids, obesity, hepatitis C
  • Recurrent disease: FSGS (~30-40% recurrence), IgA nephropathy, membranous, diabetic nephropathy, oxalosis

topics.title: Glomerular Diseases
topics.content:
Overview and Classification

Glomerular diseases are classified by:

  1. Clinical syndrome: Nephritic vs nephrotic vs mixed; asymptomatic hematuria/proteinuria
  2. Histopathologic pattern: Proliferative, membranous, sclerosing, crescentic
  3. Etiology: Primary (idiopathic) vs secondary (systemic disease)
Renal Pathology Techniques
Stain/MethodPurpose
H&EGeneral morphology
PASHighlights basement membranes, mesangial matrix
Jones (silver methenamine)Best for GBM abnormalities (spikes, double contours)
Congo redAmyloid (apple-green birefringence under polarized light)
Masson's trichromeFibrosis (collagen stains blue/green)
ImmunofluorescenceIg deposition patterns (linear vs granular vs pauci-immune)
Electron microscopyDeposit location (subepithelial, subendothelial, mesangial); GBM thickness; foot process effacement
Acute Nephritic Syndromes
Post-Streptococcal GN (PSGN)
  • Most common post-infectious GN; primarily children (6-10 years)
  • Latent period: 1-3 weeks after pharyngitis, 3-6 weeks after skin infection
  • Labs: ↓ C3 (normal C4 → alternative pathway), ↑ ASO, ↑ anti-DNase B
  • EM: Subepithelial humps ("starry sky")
  • Prognosis: Excellent in children (>95% recover); adults may develop CKD
Lupus Nephritis

ISN/RPS Classification:

ClassNameFeaturesTreatment
IMinimal mesangialNormal LM; mesangial deposits on IF/EMTreat lupus only
IIMesangial proliferativeMesangial hypercellularityLow-dose corticosteroids
IIIFocal proliferative<50% glomeruli; subendothelial depositsCorticosteroids + MMF or cyclophosphamide
IVDiffuse proliferative≥50% glomeruli; "full house" IF; worst prognosisCorticosteroids + MMF or cyclophosphamide (induction) → MMF or azathioprine (maintenance); voclosporin or belimumab added
VMembranousSubepithelial deposits; may coexist with III or IVCorticosteroids + MMF ± calcineurin inhibitors
VISclerosing>90% globally sclerosed; end-stagePrepare for RRT

"Full house" pattern: IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q deposition — characteristic of lupus nephritis (especially Class IV)

Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture's Syndrome)
  • Antibodies against α3 chain of type IV collagen in GBM (and alveolar BM)
  • Pulmonary-renal syndrome: Hemoptysis + rapidly progressive GN
  • IF: Linear IgG deposition along GBM (pathognomonic)
  • Treatment: Plasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids; prognosis depends on initial creatinine (poor if >5.7 mg/dL or requiring dialysis)
IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)
  • Most common primary GN worldwide
  • Episodic gross hematuria, often concurrent with URI ("synpharyngitic" — within 1-2 days, vs PSGN latent period)
  • Galactose-deficient IgA1 → mesangial IgA deposits
  • IF: Dominant mesangial IgA deposition
  • Complement: Normal C3 and C4 (does not consume complement via classical pathway)
  • MEST-C score (Oxford classification): Mesangial hypercellularity, Endocapillary proliferation, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis, Crescents
  • Treatment: ACEi/ARBs; corticosteroids if persistent proteinuria >1 g/day despite RAS blockade (TESTING trial); SGLT2 inhibitors; targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon — NEFIGAN trial)
  • ~20-40% progress to ESKD over 20 years
ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
DiseaseANCADistinctive Features
GPA (Wegener's)c-ANCA (PR3) ~80%Upper/lower respiratory + renal; granulomatous; saddle nose, subglottic stenosis
MPAp-ANCA (MPO) ~60%Renal + pulmonary; non-granulomatous; most common cause of pauci-immune RPGN
EGPA (Churg-Strauss)p-ANCA ~40%Asthma, eosinophilia, neuropathy; renal involvement less common
  • Renal biopsy: Pauci-immune crescentic GN (no/scant Ig deposition on IF)
  • Treatment: Induction — cyclophosphamide or rituximab + corticosteroids (RAVE, RITUXVAS trials); Maintenance — rituximab (superior to azathioprine per MAINRITSAN) or azathioprine; avacopan (C5a receptor inhibitor) as steroid-sparing agent (ADVOCATE trial)
Membranoproliferative GN (MPGN) — Reclassification
CategoryMechanismIF PatternExamples
Immune complex-mediatedClassical pathway activationIg + C3Lupus, HCV (cryoglobulinemia), infections, autoimmune
Complement-mediated (C3 glomerulopathy)Alternative pathway dysregulationC3 dominant (no/minimal Ig)C3 GN, Dense Deposit Disease (C3GN, DDD)
Nephrotic Syndrome — Primary Causes
Minimal Change Disease (MCD)
  • Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (>70%); also in adults
  • LM: Normal ("nil lesion"); EM: diffuse foot process effacement
  • Pathogenesis: T-cell derived circulating permeability factor
  • Treatment: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day × 4-8 weeks → taper; ~90% response in children
  • Frequent relapsers: Cyclophosphamide, calcineurin inhibitors, rituximab
Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)
  • Most common primary nephrotic syndrome in adults, especially African Americans (APOL1 G1/G2 risk alleles)
  • Subtypes: Not-otherwise-specified (NOS), perihilar, cellular, tip lesion (best prognosis), collapsing (worst — associated with HIV/HIVAN, COVID-19)
  • Secondary causes: Obesity, reflux, reduced nephron mass, drugs (heroin, pamidronate, interferon), viral (HIV, parvovirus B19)
  • Treatment: Corticosteroids ×4-6 months if primary; calcineurin inhibitors for steroid-resistant; rituximab; treat underlying cause if secondary
  • ~50% progress to ESKD; high recurrence post-transplant (~30-40%)
Membranous Nephropathy (MGN)
  • Most common primary nephrotic syndrome in white adults
  • Primary: Anti-PLA2R antibodies (~70%), anti-THSD7A (~3%)
  • Secondary: Malignancy (lung, colon, stomach — screen in >65 years), lupus (Class V), drugs (NSAIDs, gold, penicillamine), infections (HBV, syphilis)
  • EM: Subepithelial deposits with GBM "spike and dome" pattern (Jones stain)
  • Rule of thirds: ~1/3 spontaneous remission, ~1/3 persistent proteinuria, ~1/3 progressive renal failure
  • Treatment: Observe 6 months if low risk; rituximab (MENTOR trial — non-inferior to cyclosporine); cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids (Ponticelli regimen); calcineurin inhibitors
  • PLA2R antibody levels correlate with disease activity and predict remission
Diabetic Nephropathy
  • Leading cause of ESKD worldwide
  • Pathology: Diffuse mesangial expansion → nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules)
  • Natural history: Hyperfiltration → microalbuminuria → overt proteinuria → declining GFR → ESKD (15-25 years)
  • Treatment: ACEi/ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD), finerenone, glycemic control, BP <130/80
Glomerular Deposition Diseases
Amyloidosis
  • AL (primary): Monoclonal light chain (λ > κ); associated with myeloma/MGUS; Congo red (+), apple-green birefringence
  • AA (secondary): Serum amyloid A protein; chronic inflammation (RA, FMF, chronic infections)
  • Renal presentation: Nephrotic syndrome; enlarged kidneys (unlike most CKD)
  • Treatment: AL — target underlying plasma cell dyscrasia (bortezomib, daratumumab, stem cell transplant); AA — treat underlying inflammation; colchicine for FMF
Light Chain Deposition Disease (LCDD)
  • Monoclonal light chain deposition (κ > λ); Congo red negative
  • Nodular glomerulosclerosis (mimics diabetic nephropathy)
  • IF: Linear monoclonal light chain along GBM and TBM
Fibrillary and Immunotactoid Glomerulopathy
  • Fibrillary: Random fibrils 16-24 nm; Congo red negative; positive for DNAJB9
  • Immunotactoid: Organized microtubular deposits >30 nm; associated with lymphoproliferative disease
Basement Membrane Syndromes
Alport Syndrome
  • Mutation in type IV collagen genes: COL4A5 (X-linked — 80%), COL4A3/COL4A4 (autosomal)
  • Classic triad: Progressive GN + sensorineural hearing loss + anterior lenticonus (pathognomonic ocular finding)
  • EM: GBM "basket-weave" lamellation (splitting and lamination)
  • Males (X-linked): ESKD by 20-30 years; females: usually carrier with hematuria
  • Treatment: ACEi/ARBs to slow progression; transplantation (risk of anti-GBM disease in allograft ~3-5%)
Thin Basement Membrane Disease
  • Heterozygous COL4A3/4 mutations; GBM uniformly thin (<250 nm)
  • Presents as persistent microscopic hematuria; excellent prognosis
  • "Benign familial hematuria" — but some may progress (especially compound heterozygotes)
Glomerular-Vascular Syndromes
  • Hypertensive nephrosclerosis: Benign (arteriolar hyalinosis, intimal fibrosis) vs malignant (fibrinoid necrosis, "onion skinning")
  • Atheroembolic disease: Cholesterol clefts on biopsy; livedo reticularis, blue toes, eosinophilia, ↓ C3; after vascular procedures or anticoagulation
  • Sickle cell nephropathy: Papillary necrosis, concentrating defect, FSGS, medullary carcinoma (rare)
  • TMA: See Chapter 317
Infectious Disease-Associated GN
InfectionRenal ManifestationKey Features
HIVHIVAN (collapsing FSGS)African Americans; APOL1; large echogenic kidneys; treat with ART
HBVMembranous or MPGNTreat underlying HBV; immunosuppression may worsen viremia
HCVMPGN with cryoglobulinemiaType II mixed cryoglobulinemia; treat with DAAs
SyphilisMembranousResolves with appropriate antibiotic therapy
MalariaNephrotic syndrome (quartan malarial nephropathy)P. malariae; MPGN pattern
SchistosomiasisMPGN, amyloidosisS. mansoni most common cause

topics.title: Polycystic Kidney Disease and Other Inherited Disorders
topics.content:
Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD)
  • Most common inherited renal disease: 1:400 to 1:1000
  • PKD1 (~85%): Chromosome 16; polycystin-1; more severe, earlier ESKD (~54 years)
  • PKD2 (~15%): Chromosome 4; polycystin-2; milder, later ESKD (~74 years)
  • Two-hit hypothesis: Germline mutation (first hit) + somatic mutation (second hit) → cyst formation

Clinical Manifestations:

  • Progressive bilateral renal cysts → massive kidney enlargement
  • Flank/abdominal pain: Cyst hemorrhage, infection, or stones
  • Hematuria: Gross or microscopic; cyst rupture
  • Hypertension: Often earliest manifestation; ~60% by age 30 (before GFR decline); RAAS activation from cyst compression
  • Hepatic cysts: Most common extrarenal manifestation (~80%); more severe in women; rarely cause hepatic failure
  • Intracranial aneurysms (ICA): 4-5× general population (~8% prevalence); screen with MRA if family history of ICA or subarachnoid hemorrhage
  • Cardiac: MVP (~25%), aortic root dilation, pericardial effusion
  • Kidney stones: ~20%; uric acid and calcium oxalate (structural abnormalities impair drainage)
  • UTIs and cyst infections: More common in women; cyst infection difficult to treat (poor cyst penetration — use lipophilic antibiotics: fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX)

Diagnosis:

  • Ultrasound criteria (Pei-Ravine; for patients with PKD1 family history):
  • Age 15-39: ≥3 cysts (unilateral or bilateral)
  • Age 40-59: ≥2 cysts in each kidney
  • Age ≥60: ≥4 cysts in each kidney
  • Genetic testing: For uncertain cases, living donor evaluation, early-onset disease

Treatment:

  • BP control: Target <110/75 in young patients with preserved GFR (HALT-PKD trial); ACEi/ARBs preferred
  • Tolvaptan: V2 receptor antagonist; reduces cAMP-driven cyst growth; slows TKV increase and GFR decline (TEMPO 3:4, REPRISE trials); indicated for rapidly progressive disease; side effects: aquaresis (polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia), hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs monthly ×18 months, then q3 months)
  • Adequate hydration: Suppress vasopressin naturally
  • Avoid caffeine: Increases cAMP
  • Pain management: Avoid chronic NSAIDs; cyst aspiration/sclerotherapy for dominant painful cysts
  • RRT: Transplantation preferred; may need pre-transplant or concurrent nephrectomy for massive kidneys
Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease (ARPKD)
  • PKHD1 gene: Encodes fibrocystin/polyductin (chromosome 6)
  • Presents in neonates/infants with bilateral enlarged echogenic kidneys and congenital hepatic fibrosis (ductal plate malformation)
  • Severe neonatal form: Potter sequence (oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia) → ~30% neonatal mortality
  • Survivors: Progressive renal failure + portal hypertension (from hepatic fibrosis)
Other Inherited Cystic and Developmental Diseases
DiseaseGene/ProteinKey Features
Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC)TSC1 (hamartin)/TSC2 (tuberin) → mTOR pathwayRenal angiomyolipomas (~80%), renal cysts; cortical tubers, LAM; treat large AMLs (>4 cm) with everolimus or embolization
Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL)VHL gene (3p25)Renal cysts and clear cell RCC (bilateral, multifocal); hemangioblastomas (CNS, retina); pheochromocytoma; pancreatic cysts/tumors
ADTKD (formerly MCKD)UMOD (most common), MUC1, REN, HNF1βBland urinalysis, slowly progressive CKD, hyperuricemia/gout; NO cysts on imaging (despite old name); autosomal dominant
Nephronophthisis (NPHP)NPHP genes (>20 identified)Most common genetic cause of ESKD in children; ciliopathy; corticomedullary cysts; bland sediment; small kidneys; associated with retinitis pigmentosa (Senior-Løken), cerebellar vermis hypoplasia (Joubert)
Medullary Sponge Kidney (MSK)Unknown (usually sporadic)Dilated collecting ducts ("paintbrush" on IVP); nephrocalcinosis, recurrent stones, UTIs; benign course
CAKUTVariable (PAX2, HNF1β, etc.)Congenital anomalies; renal agenesis, dysplasia, duplex systems, PUV; most common cause of CKD in children

topics.title: Tubulointerstitial Diseases of the Kidney
topics.content:
Classification
CategorySubcategoryExamples
Acute TINDrug-inducedNSAIDs, penicillins, cephalosporins, rifampin, ciprofloxacin, PPIs, allopurinol, phenytoin, checkpoint inhibitors
Infection-associatedPyelonephritis, Legionella, Leptospira, CMV, BK virus, EBV
AutoimmuneSarcoidosis, Sjögren's, SLE, IgG4-related, TINU
Crystal-inducedUrate, oxalate, phosphate
Chronic TINReflux nephropathyVesicoureteral reflux → scarring → CKD
Analgesic nephropathyPhenacetin/NSAID combination abuse; papillary necrosis
Drug-inducedLithium (nephrogenic DI, cysts), calcineurin inhibitors (striped fibrosis), aristolochic acid (Balkan/Chinese herb nephropathy → urothelial cancer)
MetabolicHypercalcemia/nephrocalcinosis, hypokalemia, hyperuricemia
Heavy metalsLead (saturnine gout, proximal tubule dysfunction), cadmium
ObstructiveChronic obstruction → tubulointerstitial fibrosis
Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN)

Classic triad: Fever, maculopapular rash, eosinophilia — present in <10-30% of cases (especially drug-induced)

Drug-induced AIN:

  • Onset: Days to weeks after drug exposure (up to months with NSAIDs and PPIs)
  • PPIs: Increasingly recognized as common cause; insidious onset; may lack classic triad
  • Checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab): Immune-related AIN; onset 3-12 months
  • Labs: AKI, sterile pyuria, WBC casts, eosinophiluria (low sensitivity ~30%), mild proteinuria
  • Gallium-67 scan: Bilateral renal uptake (better sensitivity than eosinophiluria)

Treatment Algorithm:

  1. Discontinue offending drug (most important step)
  2. Assess response over 3-7 days
  3. If no improvement or severe AKI: renal biopsy → corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg × 2-4 weeks, then taper over 4-8 weeks)
  4. Steroid-resistant: MMF or cyclophosphamide

Indications for corticosteroids: Biopsy-proven AIN with significant interstitial inflammation, drug removed but no improvement in 5-7 days, severe AKI, dialysis-dependent

TINU Syndrome (Tubulointerstitial Nephritis and Uveitis)
  • Young women predominantly; bilateral anterior uveitis + AIN
  • May be sequential (uveitis before, during, or after nephritis)
  • Usually steroid-responsive; renal prognosis generally good
IgG4-Related TIN
  • Part of systemic IgG4-related disease (also involves pancreas, salivary glands, retroperitoneum)
  • Elevated serum IgG4; dense IgG4+ plasma cell infiltrate on biopsy; "storiform" fibrosis
  • Treatment: Corticosteroids (excellent initial response); rituximab for relapse
Crystal Deposition Nephropathies
CrystalSettingFeatures
UrateTumor lysis syndrome, chronic goutIntratubular/interstitial deposition; birefringent needle-shaped crystals; prevent with rasburicase, hydration
Calcium oxalateEthylene glycol, enteric hyperoxaluria (short bowel, gastric bypass), primary hyperoxaluria"Envelope" crystals; polarizable
Calcium phosphateIV sodium phosphate bowel prep, phosphate enemasAcute phosphate nephropathy; risk with CKD, dehydration
Drug crystalsAcyclovir, indinavir, methotrexate, sulfadiazine, ciprofloxacinVolume depletion + high dose → precipitation; prevent with hydration, dose adjustment
Light Chain Cast Nephropathy (Myeloma Kidney/LCCN)
  • Not truly TIN but classified here; intratubular obstruction by light chain casts (Tamm-Horsfall protein + monoclonal light chain)
  • "Hard" fractured casts with multinucleated giant cell reaction
  • Usually κ light chains; associated with high serum free light chain levels
  • Treatment: Treat underlying myeloma (bortezomib-based regimens); aggressive hydration; avoid volume depletion, NSAIDs, IV contrast, loop diuretics; plasmapheresis controversial
Papillary Necrosis

Causes (mnemonic: POSTCARDS): Pyelonephritis, Obstruction, Sickle cell, TB, Cirrhosis (alcoholism), Analgesic abuse, Renal transplant rejection, Diabetes, Systemic vasculitis

  • Sloughed papillae may cause ureteral obstruction ("ring shadow" on IVP)
Chronic TIN — Select Causes

Analgesic nephropathy: Chronic consumption of combination analgesics (especially phenacetin + aspirin + caffeine); papillary necrosis → CKD; increased risk of urothelial carcinoma

Aristolochic acid nephropathy: Chinese herb (Aristolochia) and Balkan endemic nephropathy; rapidly progressive CKD + urothelial carcinoma (40-50% incidence); aristolactam-DNA adducts

Lithium nephropathy: Nephrogenic DI (earliest — impaired concentrating ability), chronic TIN, microcysts, RTA; may be partially reversible with amiloride (blocks lithium entry via ENaC)

Lead nephropathy: Chronic low-level exposure; proximal tubular dysfunction, hyperuricemia, "saturnine gout"; diagnosis: EDTA mobilization test (lead-chelate excretion)

Reflux nephropathy: Most common cause of chronic TIN in children; VUR → intrarenal reflux → scarring → CKD; prevention: antibiotic prophylaxis, surgical reimplantation if severe

topics.title: Vascular Injury to the Kidney
topics.content:
Thrombotic Microangiopathies (TMA)

Common pathology: Endothelial injury → platelet activation → microvascular thrombosis → microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) (schistocytes, ↑ LDH, ↓ haptoglobin, indirect hyperbilirubinemia) + thrombocytopenia + organ injury

Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS)

Typical HUS (STEC-HUS):

  • Most common TMA; primarily children <5 years
  • Caused by Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) — usually O157:H7; also Shigella dysenteriae type 1
  • Prodrome: Bloody diarrhea → 5-10 days later: AKI, MAHA, thrombocytopenia
  • Do NOT give antibiotics to STEC infection (may increase toxin release and HUS risk)
  • Treatment: Supportive (volume, RRT if needed); ~60-70% recover renal function; 3-5% mortality

Atypical HUS (aHUS):

  • Complement-mediated: Genetic or acquired dysregulation of alternative complement pathway
  • Common mutations: Factor H (most common, ~25-30%), Factor I, Factor B, C3, MCP (CD46), THBD
  • Anti-Factor H antibodies: Especially in children; associated with CFHR1/3 deletion
  • Can be triggered by pregnancy, infections, drugs, transplantation
  • Treatment: Eculizumab or ravulizumab (anti-C5 monoclonal antibodies) — block terminal complement (MAC formation); dramatic improvement; meningococcal vaccination required before starting (risk of Neisseria meningitidis)
  • Without treatment: ~65% progress to ESKD or death within first episode
Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)
  • ADAMTS13 deficiency (<5-10% activity): Failure to cleave ultra-large vWF multimers → platelet aggregation → microvascular thrombosis
  • Acquired (immune): Anti-ADAMTS13 autoantibodies (~95% of TTP); more common in adults, women, African Americans
  • Congenital (Upshaw-Schulman syndrome): ADAMTS13 gene mutations; rare
  • Classic pentad: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + neurologic symptoms (confusion, seizures, focal deficits) + renal injury + fever — full pentad present in <5%
  • PLASMIC score: Predicts ADAMTS13 deficiency (platelet count, hemolysis markers, MCV, INR, creatinine)
  • Treatment: Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) — urgent (do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 result); caplacizumab (anti-vWF nanobody — reduces time to platelet recovery); rituximab for immune TTP; corticosteroids; platelet transfusion CONTRAINDICATED (may worsen thrombosis)
  • Without treatment: >90% mortality; with TPE: ~80-90% survival
HSCT-TMA
  • After hematopoietic stem cell transplant; multifactorial (endothelial injury, calcineurin inhibitors, infection, GVHD)
  • Treatment: Withdraw calcineurin inhibitors; eculizumab if complement-mediated; defibrotide
Other Vascular Diseases
Scleroderma Renal Crisis
  • Occurs in ~10% of systemic sclerosis (diffuse cutaneous >> limited)
  • Abrupt onset: Severe/malignant hypertension, AKI, MAHA, headaches, visual changes
  • Risk factors: Anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies, corticosteroids (>15 mg/day prednisone), early diffuse disease
  • Treatment: ACE inhibitors — first-line, life-saving (before ACEi era: >90% mortality at 1 year → now ~80% survival); captopril short-acting preferred initially for titration; continue even if dialysis required (some recover renal function months later)
  • Do NOT use ARBs (less evidence; ACEi specifically shown to improve outcomes)
HELLP Syndrome
  • Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • Complication of preeclampsia; usually third trimester
  • Treatment: Delivery (definitive); supportive care; usually resolves postpartum
Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) Nephropathy
  • TMA, renal artery/vein thrombosis, glomerular capillary thrombosis
  • Associated with lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin antibodies, anti-β2-glycoprotein I
  • Catastrophic APS: Multi-organ TMA; treatment: anticoagulation + corticosteroids + plasmapheresis ± rituximab/eculizumab
Sickle Cell Nephropathy
  • Multiple manifestations: Hyposthenuria (earliest — loss of concentrating ability), gross hematuria (papillary necrosis), FSGS, renal medullary carcinoma (rare, aggressive), CKD
  • Medullary hypoxia and sickling → vasa recta damage
  • Treatment: ACEi/ARBs for proteinuria; hydroxyurea; avoid dehydration
Renal Vein Thrombosis
  • Associated with nephrotic syndrome (especially membranous nephropathy), hypercoagulable states, malignancy
  • Presentation: Flank pain, hematuria, worsening proteinuria, ↑ creatinine; may be asymptomatic
  • Diagnosis: CT venography or MR venography
  • Treatment: Anticoagulation
Radiation Nephropathy
  • After >23 Gy to kidneys; latent period 6-12 months
  • Progressive CKD, HTN, proteinuria
  • Prevention: Shielding kidneys during radiation; ACEi may be protective

topics.title: Nephrolithiasis
topics.content:
Epidemiology
  • Lifetime prevalence: ~10-15% in men, ~5-7% in women; increasing globally
  • Peak incidence: 20-40 years
  • Recurrence: ~50% within 5-10 years without preventive measures
  • Male:female ratio narrowing (now ~1.3:1)
Stone Composition
Stone TypeFrequencyAppearanceX-ray VisibilityUrine pH
Calcium oxalate~75%Envelope-shaped crystals; dumbbell-shaped monohydrateRadiopaqueVariable
Calcium phosphate (apatite/brushite)~15%Often mixed with CaOxRadiopaqueAlkaline (>6.5)
Uric acid~8%Rhomboid/rosette crystalsRadiolucentAcidic (<5.5)
Struvite (MgNH₄PO₄)~1%"Coffin lid" crystals; staghorn calculiRadiopaqueAlkaline (>7.0)
Cystine<1%Hexagonal crystalsFaintly opaqueAcidic
Pathophysiology — Randall's Plaque Hypothesis

Calcium phosphate (apatite) deposits in the renal papillary interstitium (Randall's plaques) → erode through urothelium → serve as nidus for calcium oxalate crystallization and stone growth

Risk Factors

Dietary:

  • ↑ Risk: High sodium (↑ urinary calcium), high animal protein (↑ uric acid, ↓ citrate, ↓ urine pH), high oxalate (spinach, nuts, chocolate, rhubarb), low fluid intake, high fructose/sucrose
  • ↓ Risk: Adequate dietary calcium (binds oxalate in gut → ↓ urinary oxalate; calcium restriction INCREASES stone risk), adequate citrate (citrus fruits), adequate potassium

Urinary:

  • ↑ Risk: Low urine volume (<2 L/day), hypercalciuria (>250 mg/day in women, >300 in men), hyperoxaluria (>40 mg/day), hypocitraturia (<320 mg/day), hyperuricosuria, high/low urine pH (depending on stone type)

Medical conditions:

  • Primary hyperparathyroidism (hypercalciuria, hypercalcemia)
  • RTA Type I (alkaline urine, hypocitraturia, hypercalciuria → calcium phosphate stones, nephrocalcinosis)
  • Medullary sponge kidney
  • Gout (uric acid stones)
  • Obesity/metabolic syndrome (low urine pH → uric acid stones)
  • Inflammatory bowel disease/short bowel (enteric hyperoxaluria → calcium oxalate stones)
  • Cystinuria (autosomal recessive; SLC3A1/SLC7A9 mutations)
  • Bariatric surgery (especially Roux-en-Y — enteric hyperoxaluria)
Clinical Presentation
  • Renal colic: Sudden-onset severe flank pain radiating to groin/ipsilateral testicle or labium; colicky (waxing/waning); caused by ureteral obstruction and distension
  • Hematuria: Gross or microscopic (~85% of cases)
  • Nausea/vomiting (renal-GI reflex)
  • Urgency/frequency if stone near UVJ
Diagnostic Evaluation
  • Non-contrast helical CT: Gold standard; sensitivity >95%, specificity >98%; identifies all stone types including uric acid (radiolucent on plain film)
  • KUB radiograph: Identifies radiopaque stones; useful for follow-up
  • Renal ultrasound: Initial test in pregnancy and children; detects hydronephrosis; misses small ureteral stones
  • 24-hour urine collection: Cornerstone of metabolic evaluation (after first stone with risk factors or recurrent stones); measure volume, calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, sodium, creatinine, pH
Acute Management
  • Pain control: NSAIDs (ketorolac, ibuprofen) — first-line (reduce prostaglandin-mediated ureteral spasm); opioids if refractory
  • Medical expulsive therapy (MET): Tamsulosin (α-blocker) for distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm; may facilitate passage
  • Hydration: IV fluids if unable to maintain oral intake; no evidence that "flushing" with excess fluids helps acute passage
  • Most stones <5 mm pass spontaneously (~95%); 5-10 mm ~50%; >10 mm usually require intervention

Indications for urgent intervention: Infection with obstruction (obstructive pyelonephritis — urologic emergency), bilateral obstruction/single kidney, uncontrolled pain/vomiting, AKI

Procedural options: SWL (extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy) — best for stones <2 cm in renal pelvis/upper ureter; URS (ureteroscopy) with laser lithotripsy — stones in any location; PCNL (percutaneous nephrolithotomy) — large stones >2 cm, staghorn calculi

Prevention by Stone Type
Stone TypeSpecific Prevention
Calcium oxalateFluid intake >2.5 L/day; thiazide diuretics (chlorthalidone 25 mg, indapamide) — reduce urinary calcium by ~50%; dietary calcium 1000-1200 mg/day (NOT supplements); limit sodium <2300 mg/day; limit animal protein; potassium citrate for hypocitraturia
Calcium phosphateSimilar to CaOx; avoid excessive alkali (pH >6.5 promotes CaP); thiazides; treat underlying cause (RTA)
Uric acidUrinary alkalinization to pH 6.5 with potassium citrate (dissolves existing stones); increase fluid; low-purine diet; allopurinol or febuxostat if hyperuricemia
CystineHigh fluid intake (>3 L/day — target urine output >2.5 L); alkalinize urine to pH 7.5; low-sodium, low-methionine diet; tiopronin or D-penicillamine (chelate cystine → soluble disulfide); captopril (weak chelation)
StruviteComplete surgical removal (stones harbor bacteria); prevent UTIs; urease inhibitors (acetohydroxamic acid) — limited by side effects

topics.title: Urinary Tract Obstruction
topics.content:
Etiology
CategoryExamples
CongenitalPUV (posterior urethral valves — most common in boys), UPJ obstruction, ureterocele, ectopic ureter
Acquired IntrinsicNephrolithiasis, blood clots, papillary necrosis, urothelial tumors, strictures, fungal balls
Acquired ExtrinsicBPH (most common overall in older men), prostate/cervical/bladder cancer, retroperitoneal fibrosis, lymphadenopathy, aortic aneurysm, endometriosis, inadvertent surgical ligation
Pathophysiology

Bilateral obstruction (or unilateral in single functioning kidney):

PhaseDurationFeatures
Acute (first hours)0-1.5h↑ Renal blood flow, ↑ ureteral pressure; prostaglandin-mediated
Subacute1.5-5h↑ Ureteral pressure but ↓ renal blood flow (ATII, TXA2 vasoconstriction)
Chronic>5h to days-weeks↓ Both ureteral pressure and renal blood flow; progressive tubular damage; GFR severely reduced

Chronic obstruction: Tubulointerstitial inflammation → fibrosis → cortical thinning → irreversible nephron loss (if >4-6 weeks complete obstruction, recovery unlikely)

Clinical Features
  • Acute unilateral: Flank pain (renal colic if stone); normal renal function (contralateral kidney compensates)
  • Acute bilateral: Anuria (sudden complete) or fluctuating urine output; AKI; hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis
  • Chronic: May be painless/insidious; nocturia, polyuria (concentrating defect); recurrent UTIs; hypertension; CKD
  • BPH: Hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, incomplete emptying, overflow incontinence
  • Distal RTA (Type I): From chronic obstruction; hyperkalemic variant (Type IV)
Diagnostic Algorithm
  1. Suspect obstruction → Insert bladder catheter (rule out bladder outlet obstruction; large residual volume diagnostic)
  2. Renal ultrasound: First-line imaging; detects hydronephrosis; sensitivity ~90% for obstruction
  • False negatives: Early obstruction (<24h), dehydration, retroperitoneal fibrosis (encases ureters without dilation)
  1. Non-contrast CT: If ultrasound inconclusive; gold standard for stones and structural causes
  2. Nuclear renography (MAG3 or DTPA with furosemide): Assess functional obstruction (especially UPJ obstruction in children); T½ drainage time >20 min suggests significant obstruction
  3. Retrograde/antegrade pyelography: For precise localization; therapeutic (stent placement)
Management

Acute obstruction:

  • Relieve obstruction urgently if: Infection (obstructive pyelonephritis), bilateral/single kidney, severe AKI
  • Methods: Bladder catheterization, ureteral stent (retrograde), percutaneous nephrostomy (antegrade)
  • Treat infection with antibiotics + drainage

Postobstructive diuresis:

  • After relief of bilateral obstruction: Massive diuresis (polyuria >200 mL/h) due to accumulated urea (osmotic diuresis), sodium/water retention, impaired concentrating ability, ANP release
  • Physiologic diuresis (appropriate — excreting retained fluid/solutes) vs pathologic diuresis (inappropriate — ongoing salt/water loss beyond what was retained)
  • Management: Replace ~50-75% of urine output with 0.45% saline; monitor electrolytes q6-12h; taper replacement as output normalizes (usually 24-48 hours)
  • Risk of dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) if replacement inadequate

Chronic obstruction:

  • Treat underlying cause (BPH: α-blockers, 5α-reductase inhibitors, TURP; malignancy: stenting, nephrostomy, radiation; retroperitoneal fibrosis: corticosteroids, tamoxifen, surgery)
  • Recovery of renal function depends on duration and completeness of obstruction
  • Monitor for CKD progression even after relief

topics.title: Key Drug-Disease Associations in Nephrology
topics.content:
DrugRenal Effect
ACEi/ARBsReduce proteinuria; can cause AKI (↓ efferent tone); hyperkalemia; first-line in scleroderma renal crisis
SGLT2 inhibitorsSlow CKD progression (diabetic AND non-diabetic); initial GFR dip (hemodynamic)
TolvaptanSlows ADPKD progression; aquaresis; hepatotoxicity risk
Eculizumab/RavulizumabAnti-C5; first-line for aHUS; meningococcal vaccine required
CaplacizumabAnti-vWF; adjunct in iTTP
RituximabANCA vasculitis, membranous nephropathy, frequent-relapse MCD/FSGS, PTLD
NSAIDsPrerenal AKI, AIN, papillary necrosis, membranous nephropathy
LithiumNephrogenic DI, chronic TIN, microcysts
AminoglycosidesNephrotoxic ATN (nonoliguric, after 5-7 days)
Cyclosporine/TacrolimusAcute vasoconstriction, chronic "striped fibrosis"
CisplatinATN + salt wasting + hypomagnesemia

topics.title: Emergency Situations in Nephrology
topics.content:
EmergencyKey Action
Obstructive pyelonephritisAntibiotics + urgent drainage (stent or nephrostomy)
Hyperkalemia with ECG changesIV calcium gluconate → insulin + glucose → kayexalate/dialysis
Uremic pericarditisUrgent dialysis
TTP (suspected)Immediate plasma exchange (do NOT wait for ADAMTS13)
Scleroderma renal crisisACE inhibitor immediately
Pulmonary-renal syndromePlasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + steroids
Tumor lysis syndromeAggressive hydration + rasburicase
Anti-GBM diseasePlasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids
Ethylene glycol ingestionFomepizole, dialysis, IV thiamine + pyridoxine

Study Guide compiled from Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21st Edition — Part 9: Disorders of the Kidney and Urinary Tract (Chapters 308-319)
